blurb: Perfect violet-purple drumstick globes float on tall bare stems in early summer, humming with bees, then dry to architectural seed heads that hold for weeks.
water: Low; xeric once established, dry summer dormancy
spread: Clumping; multiplies slowly by bulb offsets and can self-seed where happy
seasons: Round purple flower globes late spring to early summer (May–Jun); strappy basal leaves emerge early and often yellow by bloom; tan seed heads persist into summer; dormant underground in winter
wildlife: Excellent pollinator magnet — the globe heads draw bees, butterflies, and other beneficial insects; the dried seed heads add winter structure
deer: Deer- and rodent-resistant — the oniony scent and taste deter deer, rabbits, voles, and squirrels, which is a major advantage over tulips and crocus
toxic: Toxic to pets — like all alliums (onion/garlic relatives), every part is poisonous to dogs, cats, and horses, and toxic to livestock in quantity; mildly irritating to people but not grown or used as food

care.hardiness: Hardy across roughly USDA zones 4–9, comfortably within Boulder's zone 5b–6a — established bulbs overwinter in the ground with no protection and are one of the most carefree bulbs for the Front Range, relishing our full sun, alkaline soil, and dry summers.[1]
care.planting: Plant the dormant bulbs in fall — mid-September to late October on the Front Range — so they root before the soil freezes.[3] Set them at a depth of about three times the bulb's height (roughly 4–6 in. for these large bulbs) in well-drained ground; on heavy clay, add coarse grit to the planting hole.[3] Choose a spot that bakes dry in summer, since the bulbs go dormant and dislike summer wet. Containers work but need a deep, fast-draining pot and winter protection from hard freeze-thaw (move pots to an unheated garage or cold frame); in-ground is far more reliable.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun — 6+ hours — for the sturdiest stems and most flowers. It tolerates light part shade but stretches and blooms less; sun also helps the bulbs ripen and dry during summer dormancy.[1]
care.soil: Well-drained, neutral-to-alkaline soil; tolerates clay, loam, and sand. Boulder's alkaline pH suits it perfectly. The one real requirement is sharp drainage — overly damp soil, especially in summer dormancy, rots the bulbs and invites fungal problems.[1]
care.water: Low. Water at fall planting and during spring growth and bloom if the season is dry, then withhold water once the foliage yellows — the bulbs want a dry, baking summer dormancy. Drought-tolerant after flowering; overwatering in summer is the main cause of bulb rot.[1]
care.spacing: Space bulbs 6–8 in. apart. For the strongest visual punch, plant in clusters or drifts of 5–15 rather than a single line; the bare stems read best rising through a low groundcover or perennials that hide the fading basal leaves.[1]
care.propagation: By offsets: mature bulbs slowly produce daughter bulbs alongside the parent — lift congested clumps in summer dormancy, separate the offsets, and replant immediately at the right depth (small offsets take a year or two to reach blooming size). By seed: it can self-sow and seed can be collected from the dried heads and sown in fall in a cold frame, but seedlings take 2–3 years to flower and named selections like 'Purple Sensation' won't come perfectly true. Division of offsets is the dependable route.[1]
care.depth: Plant bulbs about three times their height deep — roughly 4–6 in. to the base for these large bulbs — pointed end up. Deeper is better than too shallow on light soil; ensure the site drains freely.[1][2]
care.bloom: Late spring into early summer — typically May into June on the Front Range — with each globe lasting two to three weeks, then drying to a tan seed head that stays decorative for weeks longer.[1]
care.feeding: Light. A little bonemeal or low-nitrogen bulb fertilizer worked in at fall planting, and an optional light feed as growth emerges in spring, is plenty. Avoid heavy nitrogen, which favors leaves over flowers.[1]
care.maintenance: Very low. The strappy basal leaves often yellow before or during bloom — this is normal; leave them to feed the bulb and site shorter plants in front to hide them. Leave the dried seed heads for structure, or cut them once they shatter. No staking needed for these stout stems. Divide only when clumps get congested and bloom thins.[1]
care.troubles: Few. Bulb rot from poorly drained or summer-wet soil is the main risk — fix drainage. Onion-family pests (downy mildew, allium leaf miner, white rot) are uncommon in our dry climate but rotate planting sites if they appear. A real plus here: the oniony bulbs are ignored by the voles, squirrels, and deer that ravage other fall bulbs.[1]
care.companions: Combines beautifully with sun-loving, low-water perennials whose foliage masks its fading leaves — catmint, salvia, hardy geranium, ornamental grasses, and bearded iris all share its full-sun, well-drained, dry-summer needs.[1]

edible.summary: Although it is a true onion relative and smells oniony, ornamental allium is grown for its flowers, not as food — and every part is toxic to dogs, cats, and horses (and to livestock in quantity).[1][2]
edible.caution: Allium hollandicum is in the onion/garlic genus (Allium) and contains the same organosulfur compounds (e.g. N-propyl disulfide) that make all alliums dangerous to pets and livestock: ingestion by dogs, cats, or horses causes vomiting, drooling, lethargy, diarrhea, and — more seriously — the breakdown of red blood cells (Heinz-body/hemolytic anemia), which can show as weakness, blood in the urine, rapid heart rate, and panting. Keep bulbs, foliage, flowers, and seed heads away from pets and grazing animals. For people: this is an ornamental selection bred for show, not a culinary onion — it is not used as food, the bulbs and foliage are unpalatable and can cause stomach upset, and (as with the whole genus) eating large amounts is harmful. If a pet ingests any part, call ASPCA Animal Poison Control (888-426-4435) or your veterinarian; for people call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222).[1][2]
